title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Type 1 Credit Prices Continue WoW Rise; Trade Volumes Rebound After Last Week's Decline
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(28th July â€“ 3rd August 2025)
  • A total of 478,547 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 22.17% rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a 3.44% rise WoW as 1,099,410 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $52.68/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 4.09% rise WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell marginally, by 0.84% in price, reaching $53.08/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 8.60% WoW; on 5th August, the spot price stood at $58.75/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $60.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices remained unchanged last week. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 1.71%, 2.56% and 2.52% WoW, respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the past week, both Type 1 trade prices and spot prices saw an increase. Senators Amy Klobuchar and Jerry Moran introduced the Agricultural Biorefinery Innovation and Opportunity (Ag BIO) Act. The bill seeks to enhance the Biorefinery, Renewable Chemical, and Biobased Products Assistance program by updating the loan guarantee program and reinstating grants to support the development of advanced biofuels, renewable chemicals, and biobased products. Industry leaders have expressed support for the legislation, noting its potential to boost manufacturing, create jobs, and open new opportunities for farmers. According to the California Energy Commission, in the second quarter of 2025, Californians purchased 100,671 zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), which represented about 21.6% of all new vehicle sales in the state. While this marks a small decline compared to the same period in 2024, it still brings the total number of ZEVs sold in California to over 2.3 million. Despite some challenges, such as tariff disruptions, the reduction of ZEV incentives, and issues with federal funding for EV charging infrastructure, the state continues to show strong interest in zero-emission vehicles.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

Note: The total value in the table is derived from cCarbon's calculations based on weekly credit transfer data from CARB. [caption id="attachment_601826" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.09% WoW rise, increasing from $50.61 to $52.68 this week, and all non-zero credit prices decreased by 0.84% WoW, from $53.53 to $53.08. Type 1 trades saw 478,547 credits exchanged, reflecting a 22.17% rise WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a 3.44% rise WoW, reaching 1,099,410 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 8.60% WoW, reaching $57.48/MT. On 5th Â August, the spot price was $58.75/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $60.00/MT as of 5th August.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_601824" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 89 trades were reported in the market, with 10 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $75.00 for a Type 1 transaction at a volume of 13 credits, which was also the lowest transfer by volume. The lowest transfer by price was $43.60 per credit of one Type 2 transaction reported for 50,000 credits. The largest volume transfers involved 50,000 credits across four Type 2 transactions, priced at $43.60, $62.50, $64.50, and $66.50 each.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

The average spot Type 1 price stood at $55.40 in August so far, up from $47.99 in July. Trade volumes for the month of August stood at 0.12 M as of 3rd August, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_601822" align="alignnone" width="800"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 28th Â July â€“ 3rd August, total VPT recorded a fall of 1.34% WoW, with a marginal fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices remained unchanged last week. D4, D5 and D6 prices fell by 1.71%, 2.56% and 2.52% WoW, respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming Dates

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • US Senators Introduce Bipartisan Legislation to Support Biorefineries, Renewable Chemicals, and Biomanufacturing:S. Senators Amy Klobuchar (D-MN) and Jerry Moran (R-KS) introduced bipartisan legislation to enhance the ability of the Biorefinery, Renewable Chemical, and Biobased Products Assistance program to support the development of advanced biofuels, renewable chemicals, and biobased products. The Agricultural Biorefinery Innovation and Opportunity (Ag BIO) Act will update the underlying loan guarantee program and restore a grant program to support public-private partnership investment in pilot and demonstration-scale facility development.
  • Over 100,000 ZEVs Sold in California in the Second Quarter of 2025: In the second quarter of 2025, Californians purchased 100,671 zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), representing 21.6% of all new vehicle sales in the state. This marks a slight decrease in overall sales year-over-year compared to the second quarter of 2024. Despite numerous setbacks, including tariff whiplash, the dismantling of ZEV incentives, and illegal clawbacks of federal funding for electric vehicle (EV) charging infrastructure, over 100,000 ZEVs were purchased in California in Q2 of 2025, surpassing 2.3 million sales to date. Californians are making it clear: ZEVs are here to stay.
  • California Opens $55 Million Incentive Program to Expand Public Electric Vehicle Fast Charging: The California Energy Commission (CEC) announced the launch of the Fast Charge California Project, a $55 million incentive program offering up to 100% of the installation costs for electric vehicle (EV) fast chargers at businesses and public sites across the state. Applications will be accepted through Oct. 29. The program is part of the California Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Project (CALeVIP), the nationâ€™s largest EV charging incentive initiative. Since 2017, CALeVIP has supported the deployment of nearly 10,000 EV chargers, helping to accommodate the stateâ€™s more than 2.2 million light-duty EVs, according to the Center for Sustainable Energy (CSE), the programâ€™s administrator.
